name: gitcommitgate-false-positive-on-git-named-variable
tags: gitcommitgate, false positive, bash tool, regex, git commit gate, variable naming, pre-commit, task:bfccbf36
body:
GitCommitGate's regex (git\s+(?:(?!commit\b)\S+\s+)*commit\b) can false-positive on a Bash call that never invokes `git commit` at all, if the command text happens to contain the literal token "git" (e.g. a Python variable named `git`) followed later by a whole-word "commit" (e.g. inside "pre-commit", since a hyphen counts as a word boundary).

Why: the regex scans the raw Bash command string for token proximity, not actual shell command structure — it can't distinguish a real `git commit` invocation from these tokens coincidentally appearing in unrelated script content (e.g. building a markdown table description that mentions "pre-commit tests").

How to apply: when embedding Python/other scripts inside a Bash tool call, avoid naming variables `git`, and be aware that other tool descriptions mentioning both words could occasionally trip this gate too. If a Bash call is unexpectedly denied for git commit despite not running one, check the raw command string for coincidental "git ... commit" token proximity before assuming the gate itself is broken.
